graphStatsPanel.tooltipNodes: Total number of nodes (entities) in the current graph.
graphStatsPanel.tooltipEdges: Total number of relationships between nodes.
graphStatsPanel.tooltipDensity: Ratio of actual edges to the maximum possible edges. Near 0 = sparse; near 1 = fully connected. Most real-world graphs are sparse (<0.01).
graphStatsPanel.tooltipAverage: Mean number of edges per node (in + out). Higher values indicate a more interconnected graph.
graphStatsPanel.tooltipMaximum: Degree of the most connected node — the top hub. A very high max relative to average suggests a hub-and-spoke structure.
graphStatsPanel.tooltipIsolatedNodes: Nodes with no edges at all. In a data model these are orphaned entities with no modeled relationships — usually a data quality gap.
graphStatsPanel.tooltipComponents: Number of disconnected subgraphs. More than one means some nodes cannot reach others by any path. Ideally this is 1 for a unified data model.
graphStatsPanel.tooltipLargestComponent: Percentage of nodes in the biggest connected subgraph. Low values mean your data is fragmented — entities that should be related aren't linked yet.
graphStatsPanel.tooltipDiameter: Longest shortest path between any two reachable nodes. Measures how 'wide' the graph is. A high diameter means some entities are many hops apart.
graphStatsPanel.tooltipAvgPathLength: Average number of hops between all reachable pairs of nodes. Lower values indicate a tighter, more navigable graph ('small world' property).
graphStatsPanel.tooltipTopHubsByDegree: Nodes with the most connections. These are the most central entities in the graph — joins or traversals through them reach the widest set of neighbors.
graphStatsPanel.tooltipNodesByLabel: Breakdown of node count by entity type.
graphStatsPanel.tooltipEdgesByType: Breakdown of relationship count by relationship type.
